resources.string:
When BlindReader is on, it provides spoken feedback so that you can use your device without looking at the screen. This can be helpful for people who are blind or have low vision.

To navigate using BlindReader:
• Swipe right or left to move between items
• Double-tap to activate an item
• Drag two fingers to scroll

To turn off BlindReader:
• Tap the switch. You’ll see an outline. Double-tap the switch.
• On the confirmation message, tap OK. Then double-tap OK.

When BlindReader is on, it provides spoken feedback so that you can use your device without looking at the screen. BlindReader is intended for situations or people who have difficulty seeing the screen.

How to use BlindReader
• Swipe right or left to move between items
• Double-tap to activate an item
• Drag 2 fingers to scroll
How to turn off BlindReader
1. In Settings, tap Use BlindReader. There will be an outline. Double-tap it.
2. On the confirmation message, tap Stop. There will be an outline. Double-tap it.
Allow different gesture sets per application.
Current app is not available. Open the app you want to configure and return here.
Remove the per-app gesture set for the current app.
Automatically enable screen curtain when BlindReader starts.
Show a confirmation dialog before hiding the screen.
Swap the actions of left and right swipe gestures.
Use swipes from the left or right edge as shortcuts.
Allow gestures like swipe up then left or swipe left then right.
Current app unavailable. Open an app and return.
Toggle gesture set for current app

There are currently no custom labels

How to create a custom label
Navigate to an unlabeled image and add a label from the BlindReader menu
Speak notifications when screen is off
Always speak number of list items
Speak count for all scrolling methods
Cover proximity sensor to stop speech
When you select keyboard keys or navigate by character, you\'ll hear an example along with each letter, like \"h, hotel\"
After describing content, suggest how to use
Includes info while entering and exiting lists, grids, and other containers
This will reset all gestures to their default actions.\n\nYou will lose any changes that you have made.
The gesture settings have been reset.
Share a JSON backup of your settings
Save a JSON backup to a file
Paste a JSON backup to restore your settings
Choose a JSON backup file to restore
Create a named scheme from current overrides
Paste a scheme JSON and save it
Includes updates while downloading, following driving directions, and more. When turned off, you’ll still get less frequent updates.
Speak system and input windows, like when you switch to the status bar, notifications, or another keyboard
Drag your finger to hear items under your touch
Tap once on a focused item to activate
Enable enhanced navigation and controls on web pages
Prefix added before every Gemini request
Describe this screen for a blind user. Focus on UI structure, controls, and key information.
Show a movable button for quick actions
Choose what the floating button does
OCR auto-read, filters, and reading style
Describe images while reading the screen
Choose what content is spoken during read all
Choose between detailed or compact reading
Summarize this screen for a blind user. Provide a short overview and key controls.
Create action sequences and assign them to gestures
Allow gestures to trigger macro sequences
Share a JSON backup of macro actions
Paste a JSON backup to restore macro actions
Save a JSON file with macro actions
Load macro actions from a JSON file
Copy actions from one macro to another
Remove all actions from this macro?
Include the app name when announcing notifications
Allow speaking notifications while the device is locked
Suppress notification speech while read-all is active
Mute notification speech from selected apps
Let you mute notification speech per app
Notifications from this app are muted
Notifications from this app will be spoken
Open the app you want to configure and return here
Keep a history of copied text
How many recent items to keep
Choose actions shown in the quick menu
Share a JSON backup of quick menu actions
Save a JSON file with quick menu actions
Paste a JSON backup to restore quick menu actions
Load quick menu actions from a JSON file
Show the actions used right now
Share a JSON backup of app menus
Paste a JSON backup to restore app menus
Save a JSON file with app menus
Load app menus from a JSON file
Open an app to link its quick menu
No quick menu override for this app
Share the quick menu for the current app
No saved quick menu for this app
Current app menu ready to share
Paste a JSON backup to restore this app menu
Export current app menu to file
Save the current app menu as a JSON file
Import current app menu from file
Load a JSON file for the current app menu
Current app menu imported from file
Apply a saved app menu to %1$s
Unable to apply saved app menu
Turn on every quick menu action
Turn off every quick menu action
Restore the default quick menu actions
Remove all saved app quick menus
No app available to translate text
Assign actions to volume key long presses
Use volume key long presses as BlindReader shortcuts
Send manual OCR and image descriptions to a translator
Send automatic OCR and image descriptions to a translator
Describe objects when screen content changes
To use the BlindReader menu, tap the screen with 3 fingers. You can also swipe up then right, or swipe down then right.
To use the BlindReader menu, swipe down then right.
To use the reading controls, swipe left or right with 3 fingers. You can also swipe up or down with 3 fingers.
To use the reading controls, swipe up then down.
Open BlindReader at the Play Store
Get help using BlindReader or report issues

To keep using keyboard shortcuts, go to Settings and switch from the classic keymap to the default keymap.

What\'s different about the default keymap?

  • There are more navigation and web navigation actions.
  • Some of the modifier keys are different.
BlindReader is getting ready to remove the classic keymap. To keep using keyboard shortcuts, switch to the default keymap.
More information about your modifier key

For BlindReader\'s keyboard shortcuts to work correctly, update your modifier key from Search to Alt.\n\nIf you keep using the Search modifier key, some shortcuts will take different actions for this version of Android.
BlindReader\'s keyboard shortcuts will use %1$s as your modifier key.\n\nOnce you make this change, it can\'t be undone.
Modifier key has been changed to %1$s.
Search the screen for an item
Press Backspace to clear the shortcut, press Enter to save, or press Escape to cancel.
No text to copy, select some text first.
No text to paste, copy some texts first.
No text to cut, select some text first.
No text to delete, select some text first.
Are you sure you would like to reset your shortcut keys for this keymap to their default values? Please note, this action cannot be undone.
Press key combination to set new shortcut. It must contain at least ALT or Control key.
Press key combination with %1$s modifier key to set new shortcut.
This key combination is used for \'%1$s\' action. Do you want to clear it and assign this shortcut to \'%2$s\'?
Speak deleted letters in a higher-pitched voice

You can quickly adjust some of BlindReader's sound settings from any screen.

Announcement volume: Hold 1 finger on the screen and press the volume up or volume down key
Speech rate: Swipe up or down with 3 fingers
Use voice search key to print node tree instead of searching
Touch and hold delay when typing
Turn BlindReader off then on again to activate the setting.
Get automatic descriptions when you focus on an image or icon. You can also use the BlindReader menu to get more detailed AI descriptions.
Automatic descriptions for text in images
Turn on image descriptions in Settings
You can try again after turning on either automatic or detailed image descriptions
Hear longer descriptions when you select \"%1$s\" from the BlindReader menu
When using this feature, selected images will be sent to Google for processing and then deleted.\n\nGenerative AI is experimental, so it will make mistakes. By continuing, you agree to %1$s.

You'll hear a brief description when you focus on an image, like a photo in an app.

Images are processed on your device and never sent to Google. 

Generative AI is experimental, so it will make mistakes. By continuing, you agree to Google's Terms of Service.
Download needed for automatic image descriptions
Images are processed on your device and never sent to Google.\n\nGenerative AI is experimental, so it will make mistakes. By continuing, you agree to %1$s.
Downloading AI model. This may take a while. You’ll be notified when it’s complete.
Downloading still in progress. %1$d of %2$dMB downloaded.
AI model downloaded. BlindReader can now generate image descriptions.
For icons, images, and text in images
For images and text in images
For icons and text in images

Detailed image descriptions are powered by generative AI. When using this feature, selected images will be sent to Google for processing and then deleted.

Generative AI is experimental, so it will make mistakes. You can turn off detailed image descriptions anytime in BlindReader settings.

By continuing, you agree to %1$s.

You'll hear a brief description when you focus on an image, like a photo in an app.

Images are processed on your device and never sent to Google.

Generative AI is experimental, so it will make mistakes. By continuing, you agree to %1$s.
BlindReader accessibility service has hidden the screen
To change this, %1$s to open the BlindReader menu, select \"%2$s\", then double-tap.
Focus on first item on screen
Focus on last item on screen
BlindReader menu. Swipe right to explore options in the BlindReader menu.\n\nTo go back to the tutorial, use 2 fingers to swipe left or right from the edge of your screen.
Move navigation focus forward or adjust reading control down
Move navigation focus backward or adjust reading control up
Turn braille display on or off
Shows when actions exist for the current item, including copy, paste, select, and more
Open links within a focused item
Go to the next or previous page. Shows when you are reading content with multiple pages.
Shows when you select an unlabeled item
Read by character, word, lines, and more
Starting from the top, read all items on the screen
Starting from the focused item, read all items on the screen
Copy the last phrase BlindReader said to the clipboard so you can paste the text
Spell each letter of the last phrase that BlindReader said
Hear the last phrase that BlindReader said again
Enter a search term to find that text on the screen
Hide the display so your screen is not visible to others in public places. After hiding your screen, return it to a normal state.
Go to all apps and Quick Settings
Jump between spelling and grammar suggestions
Read one character at a time
Read one word at a time
Read one line at a time
Read one paragraph at a time
Jump to other windows, like volume controls or the navigation bar
Exit out of a container, like when you are in a list or grid
Jump between items you can interact with, like buttons, checkboxes, and text fields
Jump between landmarks on web pages, like \"main\" or \"navigation\"
Read one item at a time
Change whether typed keys, usage hints, grid info, and other info is read aloud
Change whether punctuation and symbols are read aloud
Change your spoken language. Shows only when you have multiple text-to-speech languages installed.
Hear sound effects as you navigate, like a beep to confirm an action
Feel vibrations for actions like selecting an item. Does not affect vibrations coming from apps or the system.
Hide the display so your screen is not visible to others when in public places
Lower volume of other audio playing when BlindReader speaks
Speak text and icons within a selected image
Shows when the cursor is on a typo
Hold finger to select any key, then double-tap
Hold finger to select letter key, then lift. Double-tap for other keys.
Hold finger to select any key, then lift
Tap to review changes to accessibility gestures
Enter the text to be used as a label for this element\nInternal view name: %1$s
Choose whether you want to override or skip existing labels in the case of conflict
Nothing to export. You have not added any labels
No applications found to import labels
No applications found to export labels.

This version of BlindReader includes changes to the default accessibility gestures. The actions associated with these gestures can be customized within BlindReader settings. When Explore by touch is enabled, you can use the following gestures to perform certain actions.\n\n%1$s
This turns off the display to improve privacy so your screen is not visible to others when in public places

This version of BlindReader includes changes to the default accessibility gestures.

The up-then-down gesture will allow you to move to the first item on the screen, and the down-then-up gesture will move to the last item.

The reading granularity can now be changed by focusing an item, then selecting a granularity from the talkback context menu.

You can change the actions performed by these gestures in BlindReader Settings.
To pause and play media, you can now double tap and hold with two fingers.\n\nOther gestures have also changed.
Open on phone to learn more

This version of BlindReader includes changes to the default accessibility gestures.\n\nThe up gesture now moves to the previous navigation setting, and the down gesture now moves to the next navigation setting.
These settings are removed from BlindReader, due to reliability issues:\n• Tap on side of device gestures\n• Shake to start continuous reading
Are you sure you want to turn off Explore by touch?\n\nYour device will no longer respond to shortcut gestures or speak items when you touch the screen.
Are you sure you want to turn on node tree debugging?\n\nNode tree debugging exposes the content of your screen to your device\'s logs, which may be read by other applications.
Are you sure you want to turn on performance statistics?\n\nPerformance statistics may consume more memory and power.
Verbose logging can aid in debugging but may cause personal information to appear in the system log buffer.\n\n Do you want to enable verbose logging?

Diagnosis mode helps debugging. Diagnosis mode overrides gesture tap with 4 fingers, to capture screen contents to system logs. Screen contents may include personal information. \n\n Do you want to enable diagnosis mode?
This helps BlindReader reduce announcements when you\'re on a call
Press arrow buttons to change value, or press select to finish.

After selecting \"Read from top\" or \"Read from focused item\", you can still navigate to the previous or next item while the text is being read. This lets you go back or skip ahead with ease.\n\nTo adjust the volume while BlindReader speaks, press the volume up or volume down key.
Use voice commands to control BlindReader

Here are some things you can say:

• Copy
• Read from focused item
• Find [save]
• Speak [faster]
• BlindReader settings

Say "%1$s" for a list of all voice commands
Skip BlindReader gestures to use system gestures
When you hear a chime, you can use a system gesture that isn\'t supported by BlindReader. This is known as a pass-through gesture.
%1$s to select a setting. Then %2$s to adjust the setting.
To use this, go to BlindReader settings and add a gesture for \'%1$s\'
To use this, go to BlindReader settings and add a gesture for spell check
Braille keyboard isn\'t turned on. Go to BlindReader settings, set up Braille keyboard, and try again.

BlindReader is Google's screen reader. It reads aloud the text on the screen and gives you spoken feedback, so that you can use your device without looking at the screen.

With BlindReader on, a selected item on the screen is spoken aloud, sometimes with additional information or context added.

Press Select to continue. The Select button is located at the center of the directional pad, which is the large, circular button at the top of your remote.

Move around the screen using the circle-shaped directional button at the top of your remote. To select, press the center of the directional button at the top of your remote.

In the second row on the left, you have the back button. On the right you have the voice Assistant button.

In the third row on the left, you have the home button. On the right is the mute button.

In the fourth row on the left, you have the YouTube shortcut. On the right is the Netflix shortcut.

In the bottom row there are two small buttons. The left button is the power button. The right will open your TV’s input menu.

The volume buttons are located at the right side of the remote.

Press Select to continue.

To access the BlindReader shortcut, press the down and back buttons at the same time and hold them down for 3 seconds. This will either turn on or turn off BlindReader.

You can also access this shortcut within your TV settings.

Press Select to continue.
You can come back to the tutorial anytime in BlindReader settings.
You can come back to review new features anytime in BlindReader settings.
To pause or play media in a video or music app, double-tap with 2 fingers. Try it now.
Other apps may use this gesture for things like answering or ending a phone call. This gesture may not be supported by all apps.
You can now hear more detailed image descriptions powered by generative AI.\n\nTo turn on this feature, focus on the image below, open the BlindReader menu, and choose \"Describe image\".
You can change this setting anytime in BlindReader settings > Image & icon descriptions.
You can change this setting anytime in BlindReader settings > Image & icon descriptions.
Note: Some languages may not be supported yet.
Sample image. Open the BlindReader menu and select \"Describe image\" to hear a detailed description.

The Google Disability support team is available to help with accessibility features and assistive tech, including BlindReader.\n\nTo check available contact methods or report an issue, go to BlindReader settings > Contact Google Disability support.

The Google Disability support team is available to help with accessibility features and assistive tech, including BlindReader.\n\nTo check available contact methods or report an issue, go to BlindReader settings then Contact Google Disability support
Learn more about Google Disability support
More verbosity options for punctuation & symbols

You can now control whether BlindReader speaks all punctuation and symbols, most, or some. To select the level of detail that you prefer, go to BlindReader settings > Verbosity > Speak punctuation and symbols.\n\nTip: You can also use reading controls to quickly change how BlindReader speaks punctuation and symbols as you navigate. To set this up, go to Customize menus > Customize reading controls.

You can now control whether BlindReader speaks all punctuation and symbols, most, or some. To select the level of detail that you prefer, go to BlindReader settings then Verbosity then Speak punctuation and symbols.\n\nTip: You can also use reading controls to quickly change how BlindReader speaks punctuation and symbols as you navigate. To set this up, go to Customize menus then Customize reading controls.
Media paused or playing. Usually, this will pause or play the current video or music. Swipe right to continue.
To stop BlindReader from talking temporarily, tap with 2 fingers.
BlindReader speech stopped. Usually, this will stop the current phrase that BlindReader is saying. Swipe right to continue.
To copy text, double-tap with 3 fingers.
Copied text. Usually, this will copy the text you have selected. Swipe right to continue.
To paste text, triple-tap with 3 fingers.
Pasted text. Usually, this will paste the text you have copied. Swipe right to continue.
To cut text, double-tap and hold with 3 fingers.
Cut text. Usually, this will cut the text you have selected. Swipe right to continue.
To start or end text selection mode, double-tap and hold with 2 fingers.
Selection mode on or off. Usually, this will start or stop text selection mode when you are inside an input field. Swipe right to continue.
To use the reading controls, swipe left or right with 3 fingers. You can also swipe up or down with 3 fingers.
Note: You have customized some of your gestures, so this information may not be accurate. To review your gestures, go to BlindReader settings.
New gestures have been added to make BlindReader faster and easier to use.\n\nOn this screen, you can try the new gestures. You can review this information in BlindReader settings at any time.
Tap to learn how to use BlindReader faster with new gestures.
Node tree has been printed to logs.
Here are gestures performed recently, please report which gesture is wrong and what is expected.
More verbosity options for punctuation & symbols
You will learn more about these changes in the next few screens.\n\nYou can review this information later in BlindReader settings > New features.
You will learn more about these changes in the next few screens.\n\nYou can review this information later in BlindReader settings then New features.
To customize the gesture that is used for image descriptions, go to BlindReader settings > Customize gestures.
To customize the gesture that is used for image descriptions, go to BlindReader settings then Customize gestures.
You can use the following commands and gestures to select text more quickly.
Select from cursor to beginning of text box: Press space and dots 1, 2, 3, 7, 8
Select from cursor to end of text box: Press space and dots 4, 5, 6, 7, 8
Select from cursor to beginning of text box: Hold dots 4 and 5, then swipe up anywhere with 2 fingers
Select from cursor to end of text box: Hold dots 4 and 5, then swipe down anywhere with 2 fingers
You can review the full list of commands in BlindReader settings.
BlindReader reads content on your screen aloud and changes how navigation works on your device. To turn it off, tap “Turn off BlindReader” 2 times.

BlindReader provides spoken feedback so that you can use your device without looking at the screen. BlindReader can be helpful for people who have difficulty seeing the screen. 

If you want to quickly turn BlindReader on or off using your volume keys, you can set up a shortcut in BlindReader settings > BlindReader shortcut.

When BlindReader is on, the focused item on the screen is spoken out loud.

Swiping right will move to the next item, and swiping left will move to the previous item.

Swipe right now to find the next button.

BlindReader provides spoken feedback so that you can use your device without looking at the screen. BlindReader can be helpful for people who have difficulty seeing the screen. 

If you want to quickly turn BlindReader on or off using your volume keys, you can set up a shortcut in BlindReader settings then BlindReader shortcut.

When BlindReader is on, the focused item on the screen is spoken out loud.

Swiping right will move to the next item, and swiping left will move to the previous item.

Swipe right now to find the next button.
Swipe right now to find the next button
You can also focus on an item by dragging your finger across the screen.\n\nTry this now by touching the screen and dragging toward the bottom left corner until you find the next button.
Scroll by swiping up or down with 2 fingers
You can practice on this screen until you find the \"Next\" button.
Try scrolling by swiping up or down with 2 fingers
Great job! You\'ve scrolled to the bottom of the screen.\n\nSwipe right to find the next button.

This means when using BlindReader, you can go to the home screen by swiping up from the bottom with 2 fingers instead of 1.\n\nTrying these gestures now will close the tutorial. You may want to wait until you have completed the tutorial.
To go to your home screen, swipe up from the bottom of your screen with 2 fingers.
To go to your recent apps, swipe up from the bottom of your screen with 2 fingers and hold. Then release. This takes you to the overview screen.
To go back, start at the left or right edge and swipe towards the middle of your screen with 2 fingers.
To open notifications, swipe down from the top of the screen with 2 fingers.
To open notifications, swipe down from the top of the screen with 2 fingers. Then you can close notifications by swiping back up with 2 fingers.\n\nTry it now.
To open your accessibility shortcut, swipe up from the bottom of the screen with 3 fingers.
The volume for BlindReader speech is called the accessibility volume. It\'s adjusted separately from the media volume.\n\nTo adjust the accessibility volume:
While holding, press the volume up or volume down key on the side of your device
You\'ll hear the new accessibility volume after a few seconds.\n\nTry adjusting the accessibility volume now.
The BlindReader menu includes settings and controls that you may need often.\n\nOpen the BlindReader menu now by tapping the screen with 3 fingers.
The BlindReader menu includes settings and controls that you may need often.\n\nOpen the BlindReader menu now by swiping down then right.

These shortcuts can help you quickly change settings, like switching your navigation from characters to words or changing BlindReader's speech rate.

To quickly change your reading controls, swipe left with 3 fingers or swipe right with 3 fingers.

You can customize the BlindReader menu and your reading controls later in BlindReader settings.

These shortcuts can help you quickly change settings, like switching your navigation from characters to words or changing BlindReader's speech rate.

To quickly change your reading controls, swipe down and then back up.

You can customize the BlindReader menu and your reading controls later in BlindReader settings.
You are ready to use BlindReader.\n\nKeep learning about other topics now, or review this info anytime in BlindReader settings.
Swipe right to select a topic. For the best experience, turn on BlindReader before getting started.
Move between items, scroll, use menus, and more
Type, select, edit, copy, and more
Read by word or character, and jump between links, controls or headings
Learn how to make calls, send messages, and more
Try different gestures to learn what they do
Learn more about BlindReader and send feedback

When you activate a text box, a keyboard opens at the bottom of your screen.

To dismiss the keyboard, double-tap the back button in the bottom corner of your screen.

Try activating and dismissing the text box below.
Move your finger across the keyboard. When you hear the letter you want to type, lift your finger.\n\nTry activating the text box below and type your name.
You can also type with the braille keyboard. Find it in BlindReader settings.
When you have activated a text box, you can move the cursor between letters by swiping up or down.\n\nTry it in the text box below.

In a text box, move the cursor to the beginning of the text you want to select.

Double-tap and hold with 2 fingers to start selection mode. Then move the cursor to the end of the text. Double-tap and hold with 2 fingers again to end selection mode.

Try to select the word "Welcome" in the text box below.

In a text box, move the cursor to the beginning of the text you want to select.

Swipe down and then right to open the BlindReader menu. Double-tap actions and then start selection mode. Move the cursor to the end of the text.

Open the BlindReader menu again, then double-tap actions to end selection mode.

Try to select the word "Welcome" in the text box below.
After selecting text, copy it by double tapping with 3 fingers.\n\nTry to copy a word in the text box below.

To cut text, double-tap and hold with 3 fingers.

To paste text, triple-tap with 3 fingers.

Try to paste a word in the text box below.
After selecting text, you can copy it in the BlindReader menu. Swipe down and then right to open the BlindReader menu. Double-tap actions and then copy.\n\nTry to copy a word in the text box below.
To cut or paste text, open the BlindReader menu and double-tap actions.\n\nTry to paste a word in the text box below.

You can use proofreading to check the spelling or grammar within a text box.\n\nTo try it, focus on the text box below, which contains spelling errors. Then, swipe right with 3 fingers until you find \"Proofread\".

You can use proofreading to check the spelling or grammar within a text box.\n\nTo try it, focus on the text box below, which contains spelling errors. After focusing, swipe down and then up until you find \"Proofread\".

You can use proofreading to check the spelling or grammar within a text box.\n\nTo try it, focus on the text box below and type a misspelled word. Then, swipe right with 3 fingers until you find \"Proofread\".

You can use proofreading to check the spelling or grammar within a text box.\n\nTo try it, focus on the text box below and type a misspelled word. After focusing, swipe down and then up until you find \"Proofread\".
Read by character, word, or paragraph

Sometimes, you might want to hear text read aloud one character at a time.

To do this, swipe right with 3 fingers until you hear "%1$s". Next, swipe up to move the navigation focus backward by character. Swipe down to move the navigation focus forward by character.

Try it now. When you swipe right with 3 fingers, you can also choose to read by words or paragraphs.

Sometimes, you might want to hear text read aloud one character at a time.

To do this, swipe down and then up until you hear "%1$s". Next, swipe up to move the navigation focus backward by character. Swipe down to move the navigation focus forward by character.

Try it now. When you swipe down and then up, you can also choose to read by words or paragraphs.

Controls are items that you can interact with, like buttons, checkboxes, and text boxes.

On some pages, you may want to jump between controls to quickly find an action, like the next button.

To do this, swipe right with 3 fingers until you hear "%1$s". Next, swipe up to go to the previous control on a page. Swipe down to go to the next control on a page.

Try it now. Switch to controls and find the next button.

Controls are items that you can interact with, like buttons, checkboxes, and text boxes.

On some pages, you may want to jump between controls to quickly find an action, like the next button.

To do this, swipe down and then up until you hear "%1$s". Next, swipe up to go to the previous control on a page. Swipe down to go to the next control on a page.

Try it now. Switch to controls and find the next button.

You can also jump between links on a page.

To try it now, swipe right with 3 fingers until you hear "%1$s". Next, swipe down to find the link named "continue". While in the tutorial, double tapping this link will not do anything.

Swipe right to find the next button and go to the next page.

You can also jump between links on a page.

To try it now, swipe down and then up until you hear "%1$s". Next, swipe down and find the link named "continue". While in the tutorial, double tapping this link will not do anything.

Swipe right to find the next button and go to the next page.

You can also jump between headings on a page.

To try it now, swipe right with 3 fingers until you hear "%1$s". Next, swipe down to find the "navigation" heading.

To finish the tutorial, swipe right to find the finish button.

You can also jump between headings on a page.

To try it now, swipe down and then up until you hear "%1$s". Next, swipe down to find the "navigation" heading.

To finish the tutorial, swipe right to find the finish button.
Swipe right to find the finish button.

You can use your voice to control BlindReader quickly.

For example, say a command to change navigation, jump to the next heading, copy text, and more.

To try it now, swipe right and then up.
You can call a family member, friend, or business with the phone app on your home screen.\n\nWhen you receive a call, you can double-tap with 2 fingers to answer it.
You can also ask Google Assistant to call a number or the name of someone in your contacts
To send a message, go to your home screen and find a messaging app
You can also ask Google Assistant to send a message to a contact or number
Reading mode makes long content easier to navigate, listen to, and read. You can change font size and contrast, have the text read aloud, and more.

After installing Reading mode:

1. Go to Settings > Accessibility > Reading mode
2. Turn on the shortcut
3. Go to an app or website, and use the shortcut to open Reading mode

After installing Reading mode:

1. Go to Settings then Accessibility then Reading mode
2. Turn on the shortcut
3. Go to an app or website, and use the shortcut to open Reading mode
Exploring items and text around you
Lookout uses your camera to get details about objects, images, and text around you. You can use it to read signs, sort mail, and more.
Lookout has several modes, depending on the task you’re trying. For example, to scan a page of text, select documents mode.
BlindReader will announce when you get a new notification.
First, swipe down with 4 fingers to access the notification.
Then, swipe left or right to navigate within the notification.
You can check which apps have notifications turned on in Settings > Notifications.
You can check which apps have notifications turned on in Settings then Notifications.
To check your notification history, swipe down with 2 fingers from the top of your screen

Try any BlindReader gesture on this screen to hear what that gesture does.

Tap the screen with 2 fingers. Keep practicing by tapping once, twice, or three times with 2, 3, or 4 fingers.

You can also double-tap and hold with 2, 3, or 4 fingers.

Try any BlindReader gesture on this screen to hear what that gesture does.\n\nTry any gesture where two swipes form a right angle. For example, swipe down and then right, without lifting your finger in between.
This tutorial works best with BlindReader on. You can make this change in Settings.\n\nIf you continue with BlindReader off, some of the tutorial may not work as intended.
To practice gestures, first turn on BlindReader

When BlindReader is on, the focused item on the screen is spoken out loud.

Swiping right moves to the next item. Swiping left moves to the previous item.

Swipe right now until you find the next button.
Swipe up or down with 2 fingers
When you want to go back, use two fingers to swipe right from the edge of the screen
To open settings and controls you may need often, swipe down and then right
You\'re ready to start using BlindReader
You\'ll notice some bug fixes and changes to how the focus moves
Please check if the phone is connected.
Image descriptions are not available when the screen is hidden. Show your screen and try again.
Download needed for automatic icon descriptions
%1$d MB using mobile data. Data charges may apply.
You\'ll hear a brief description when you focus on an icon, like a button in an app.\n\nImages are processed on your device and never sent to Google.
Download needed for automatic image descriptions
BlindReader can recognize and describe images on the screen. This can help identify photos in apps and more.
BlindReader can still describe text in images
Storage is full. To download, free up %1$d MB and try again.
Cannot download. Check your connection and try again.
Hear descriptions when you focus on an icon
Hear descriptions when you focus on an image
Hear descriptions when you focus on an image that contains text
You can still get descriptions for a selected image from the BlindReader menu
To get descriptions again later, you can download this feature from BlindReader settings
Turn on to enable screen understanding with Gemini
To hear image descriptions, get the latest version of %1$s from Google Play.\n\nOnce download is complete, come back to BlindReader settings and turn on automatic image descriptions again.
No internet connection. Check your Wi-Fi or mobile network and try again.
